title: Listening - Part 3: A Neighbourhood Project
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about why they became involved in a neighbourhood project. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the main reason each speaker gives for getting involved. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about why they became involved in a neighbourhood project. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the main reason each speaker gives for getting involved. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five people talk about taking part in a neighbourhood project.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: I moved into this neighbourhood last autumn and knew only the person who lived next door. Since I work from home, weeks could pass without my speaking to anyone nearby. Then I saw a notice for a community garden project. [19]I volunteered because it seemed a natural way to meet people who lived in the same area while doing something together, rather than trying to introduce myself to strangers for no reason.[/19] The garden is beginning to look good, but the real success for me is recognising people whenever I go out.||Speaker 2 Man: Behind our houses there is a narrow area of grass where wild flowers used to grow. Recently people began cutting everything very short, and I noticed far fewer butterflies and bees there in summer. [20]I became involved in a planting scheme because I wanted to provide suitable flowers and shelter for insects and birds that were disappearing from that small local space.[/20] Some residents initially wanted neat grass instead, but once the flowers appeared, most agreed that it looked better as well.||Speaker 3 Woman: During a particularly cold winter, I heard that several older people on our street were having difficulty shopping and collecting medicine. Their families didn't all live nearby, and icy pavements made going out risky. [21]I joined a neighbourhood support group because I wanted residents who were less mobile to have someone local they could call for groceries or a brief visit when they needed help.[/21] We still run the system year-round now, since sometimes company is as valuable as shopping.||Speaker 4 Man: There's a pedestrian passage beside the railway station which almost everyone uses, but for years it was covered in graffiti and surrounded by broken fencing. People hurried through it because it felt neglected. [22]I joined the art and clean-up project because I wanted to transform a depressing public space into somewhere residents could walk through without feeling embarrassed about the condition of their town.[/22] We removed rubbish and painted murals. It hasn't solved every problem, but people now stop to take photographs there.||Speaker 5 Woman: Cars often drive too fast outside our primary school, particularly at the time children are arriving. I don't have children there myself, but I cycle past every morning and had seen several dangerous situations. [23]I joined the residents' campaign because I wanted crossings and lower speed limits introduced before a child or cyclist was seriously injured on that road.[/23] It took months of meetings, but new signs and a crossing have finally appeared. Drivers seem more cautious already, which makes every discussion worthwhile.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: I moved into this neighbourhood last autumn and knew only the person who lived next door. Since I work from home, weeks could pass without my speaking to anyone nearby. Then I saw a notice for a community garden project. I volunteered because it seemed a natural way to meet people who lived in the same area while doing something together, rather than trying to introduce myself to strangers for no reason. The garden is beginning to look good, but the real success for me is recognising people whenever I go out.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: Behind our houses there is a narrow area of grass where wild flowers used to grow. Recently people began cutting everything very short, and I noticed far fewer butterflies and bees there in summer. I became involved in a planting scheme because I wanted to provide suitable flowers and shelter for insects and birds that were disappearing from that small local space. Some residents initially wanted neat grass instead, but once the flowers appeared, most agreed that it looked better as well.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: During a particularly cold winter, I heard that several older people on our street were having difficulty shopping and collecting medicine. Their families didn't all live nearby, and icy pavements made going out risky. I joined a neighbourhood support group because I wanted residents who were less mobile to have someone local they could call for groceries or a brief visit when they needed help. We still run the system year-round now, since sometimes company is as valuable as shopping.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: There's a pedestrian passage beside the railway station which almost everyone uses, but for years it was covered in graffiti and surrounded by broken fencing. People hurried through it because it felt neglected. I joined the art and clean-up project because I wanted to transform a depressing public space into somewhere residents could walk through without feeling embarrassed about the condition of their town. We removed rubbish and painted murals. It hasn't solved every problem, but people now stop to take photographs there.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: Cars often drive too fast outside our primary school, particularly at the time children are arriving. I don't have children there myself, but I cycle past every morning and had seen several dangerous situations. I joined the residents' campaign because I wanted crossings and lower speed limits introduced before a child or cyclist was seriously injured on that road. It took months of meetings, but new signs and a crossing have finally appeared. Drivers seem more cautious already, which makes every discussion worthwhile.

options.text: to share knowledge with younger people
to meet people after moving house
to provide support for elderly residents
to learn how local decisions are made

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: The speaker joined the garden project as a way to get to know people in a new neighbourhood (evidence [19]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: The speaker joined the planting project in order to support local birds and insects (evidence [20]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: The speaker helps in order to support older local residents with practical needs and companionship (evidence [21]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: The speaker wanted to turn a neglected passage into an attractive public space (evidence [22]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The speaker campaigned for road changes to reduce danger near the school (evidence [23]).
